text.title: XP Practices and Simple Design Rules
text.summary: 12 XP practices with descriptions, solo-dev applicability ratings, and Kent Beck's four rules of simple design.

section.title: Kent Beck's Four Rules of Simple Design (Priority Order)

rule.statement: Apply Kent Beck's four rules in strict priority order: (1) passes all tests, (2) reveals intention, (3) no duplication, (4) fewest elements. A design that passes tests but is unreadable is better than one that is readable but fails tests; a design with duplication that passes tests is better than no-duplication code that does not.
rule.rationale: The priority order resolves conflicts. Agents frequently violate rule 4 (fewest elements) by adding interfaces, strategies, and factories preemptively. The rule is the concrete, testable definition of over-engineering.

list.item: Rule 1: Passes all tests — correctness is the non-negotiable baseline.
Rule 2: Reveals intention — names, structure, and comments communicate purpose without ambiguity.
Rule 3: No duplication (DRY) — every piece of knowledge has one authoritative location.
Rule 4: Fewest elements — no classes, methods, or interfaces that are not currently needed (YAGNI).
Planning Game: customer prioritizes stories, team estimates; stories > 13 points are split.
Small Releases: internal every iteration (1-2 weeks), external monthly or more frequently.
Metaphor: shared vocabulary for the system domain; prevents naming drift.
Simple Design: rules 1-4 above applied continuously, not just at start.
TDD: failing test before production code, always. Non-negotiable.
Refactoring: continuous design improvement in dedicated commits separate from feature changes.
Pair Programming: two developers, one computer. For solo+AI: Claude as pair partner.
Collective Ownership: any developer can change any code; requires strong standards + tests.
Continuous Integration: integrate and run full test suite multiple times daily.
Sustainable Pace: 40-hour weeks; tired developers make mistakes that cost more than the hours saved.
On-site Customer: real decision-maker available for questions; PMs as proxies are acceptable.
Coding Standards: consistent style across all code; enforced by pre-commit hooks.
TDD — essential; run with Claude as the pair that writes failing tests first.
Simple Design — essential; use rule 4 to reject agent-generated abstractions without 2+ concrete uses.
Small Releases — essential; CI/CD automates the release gate.
Continuous Integration — essential; GitHub Actions / GitLab CI is the substrate.
Refactoring — essential; always in a separate commit from the feature change.
Pair Programming — adapted; Claude drives the coding, human navigates and reviews.
On-site Customer — adapted; async feedback via issue comments or recorded demos.
Collective Ownership — not applicable solo; but use it to resist siloing knowledge in one module.
